dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 7 for an active seizure call at a residence in Richmond.
Hi, I'm Ben, an EMT with the rescue squad. What prompted you to call 911 today?
She's having a seizure, my sister. She was swimming in the pool and started acting strangely. We pulled her out.
Ma'am, can you open your eyes? Can you hear me? Can you move your fingers?
She's not opening her eyes. Eye movement is absent to stimuli.
No verbal response-making this a GCS of 3.
She is demonstrating continuous generalized motor activity with no vocal response.
Airway is patent and not currently compromised despite seizure activity.
Respiratory effort is irregular, with a high rate and audible sounds; she's using accessory muscles.
Radial pulses are strong, equal, and regular. Skin is warm, dry, and has normal color.
Pulse is 119-elevated and irregular when checked manually.
Respiratory rate is 42, extremely fast and inconsistent.
Blood pressure is 125 over 72. No signs of hypotension yet.
Blood glucose is 193 mg/dL-no immediate concern here.
SpO₂ is 97%. Oxygen saturation is still acceptable.
EKG is showing sinus rhythm with no arrhythmias present.
Applying 4-lead cardiac monitoring for ongoing assessment.
Setting up capnography now to monitor respiratory pattern and airway status.
Could you clarify when and how the seizure appeared? Any red flags?
In the pool, head was up, rigid, drooling-then progressed to tonic-clonic seizure, about 15 minutes before we arrived.
Has she had past seizures or known health conditions?
She has monthly absence seizures and is compliant with Dilantin and Depakote.
Any known allergies we should be aware of?
She's allergic to penicillin-nothing else.
Pupils are 2 mm, non-reactive with a slight right-side gaze and some drooling observed.
Skin is still warm and dry with normal cap refill-no rashes or discoloration.
Chest is rising and falling normally with clear bilateral breath sounds. No wheeze or crackles noted.
Abdomen is soft and non-tender with no signs of guarding or masses.
Following midazolam, pupils became reactive, now 4 mm. We also noted some myoclonus earlier.
Rechecking-no pallor, and skin remains dry and warm.
Administering 5 mg of midazolam IM in the left deltoid to stop the seizure.
Pulse is 97 now. Showing improvement after drug administration.
Respiratory rate is 11-more regular in pattern now and is within concerns.
Blood pressure is 106 over 69-stable but slightly lower.
SpO₂ remains stable at 99%.
Medication appears to be effective. Seizure has stopped, and she is responding to voice commands now.
Establishing an 18-gauge IV via the external jugular vein now.
Based on findings, the medical seizure protocol (3-12) is the selected course of action.
Final pulse is 96-showing continued normalization.
Respiratory rate is 19-staying within normal expected range.
Blood pressure is 104 over 66-showing trending decrease but still stable.
SpO₂ is at 100%-very well oxygenated and stable.
Chippenham Hospital has been selected as the receiving facility. Pre-arrival report has been sent.
She is being placed in a semi-Fowler's position for transport. Shoulders and lap belts are engaged.
Capnography and EKG monitoring remain active and on board for transportation.
All pre-transport patient status information is updated and ready for emergency department.
Rechecking-chest rise remains stable with equal and clear bilateral breath sounds.
Abdomen continues to appear soft without guarding or tenderness.
No skin lesions, rashes, or discoloration found upon reassessment.
Medical protocol 3-12 for seizure has been followed completely.
Integrated monitoring and documentation are completed for transport.
EMS team is preparing the patient's stretcher for ambulance loading and travel.
Patient remains non-verbal but appears stable during all transport preparations.
All systems are verified secure and ready for motion protocols during transit.
ED has been fully briefed on the patient's status and clinical condition with transportation progress.
EKG still shows normal sinus rhythm-no changes on recheck.
All transport procedures are complete and safe for in-vehicle movement.
Hospital is aware of current status, and transfer is confirmed.
Full verification checks are completed for ambulance system readiness.
BP remains within a normal range for transport-with no deviation observed.
Motion monitoring systems are active and verified. No safety violations reported.
Pulse remains at 96 with consistent, stable rhythm during motion.
Respiratory effort is maintained at 19-no concerns noted during transit assessment.
All components of protocol 3-12 have been implemented as per medical guidelines.
Patient is sufficiently secured and system checks are in compliance for transport.
Ambulance is operational, and destination protocols are completed without incident.
Final transition report and care packet handed off to receiving staff.
She also has mild anemia in her history-no other significant conditions.
All transport documentation and handoff procedures are complete.
Ready for ambulance transportation and monitoring procedures.
Blood pressure remains 104/66-within acceptable transport parameters.
Transport procedures are online and verified for motion readiness.
Pulse remains at 96-normal trend during motion.
Respiratory rate is 19 and regular during passenger movement.
Midazolam remains effective; patient is quieter and more responsive.
All protocol steps completed and documented prior to hospital turn-over.
Receiving facility has all necessary clinical and care instructions.
SpO₂ is maintaining 100%-consistent.
Patient secured and motion systems active-ready for ambulance motion.
All preparatory steps confirmed completed for transport.
All aspects of care have been finalized for transfer protocols.
EMS team all set for motion transition and ambulance departure.
We checked for pain and do not see signs of acute distress.
The patient is now responsive to verbal commands and is following them.
Her respiratory rate has settled at 11 and is stable.
No pain behaviors observed. Pain assessed as zero on the scale.
Her pallor was not noted through assessment.
